title: Introduction to ellipses
description: The ellipsis is used to show a pause in speech or that part of a sentence has been removed.
topic_path: Punctuation: the colon, semicolon, and more

assessment_items.question_content:
**Choose the best way to paraphrase the quotation.**

>"The whole of life, from the moment you are born to the moment you die, is a process of learning.” - *Jiddu Krishnamurti*

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We can use an **ellipsis** to cut out parts of a quotation:

* *"I ran around the track incredibly fast."*
* *"I ran . . . incredibly fast."*

However, we shouldn't just cut anything out. When paraphrasing, it's important to keep the *meaning* of the quotation basically the same. 

What we *don't* want is for the paraphrased quote to be confusing or missing critical information.

The paraphrased quotation that makes sense and keeps its original meaning is

>**"The whole of life . . . is a process of learning."**

choices.content: "The whole of life, from . . . to the moment you die, is a process of learning."
"The whole of life . . . is a process of learning."
". . . the moment you are born . . . is a process of learning."

assessment_items.question_content:
**Choose the best way to paraphrase the quotation.**

>"Anyone who stops learning is old, whether at twenty or eighty. Anyone who keeps learning stays young.” - *Henry Ford*

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We can use an **ellipsis** to cut out parts of a quotation:

* *"I ran around the track incredibly fast."*
* *"I ran . . . incredibly fast."*

However, we shouldn't just cut anything out. When paraphrasing, it's important to keep the *meaning* of the quotation basically the same. 

What we *don't* want is for the paraphrased quote to be confusing or missing critical information.

The paraphrased quotation that makes sense and keeps its original meaning is

>**"Anyone who stops learning is old . . . Anyone who keeps learning stays young.”**

choices.content: "Anyone who stops learning is old . . . Anyone who keeps learning stays young.”
"Anyone who stops learning is . . . twenty or eighty.”
"Anyone who stops learning . . . stays young.”

assessment_items.question_content:
**Choose the best way to paraphrase the quotation.**

>"If you want to be a writer, you must do two things above all others: read a lot and write a lot.” - *Stephen King*

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We can use an **ellipsis** to cut out parts of a quotation:

* *"I ran around the track incredibly fast."*
* *"I ran . . . incredibly fast."*

However, we shouldn't just cut anything out. When paraphrasing, it's important to keep the *meaning* of the quotation basically the same. 

What we *don't* want is for the paraphrased quote to be confusing or missing critical information.

The paraphrased quotation that makes sense and keeps its original meaning is

>**"If you want to be a writer, you must . . . read a lot and write a lot."**

choices.content: "If you want to be a writer, you must do two things above all others . . ."
"If you want to . . . do two things above all others: read a lot and write a lot."
"If you want to be a writer, you must . . . read a lot and write a lot."

assessment_items.question_content:
**Choose the best way to paraphrase the quotation.**

>"Instead of letting books grow moldy behind an iron grating, far from the vulgar gaze, it is better to let them wear out by being read.” - *Jules Verne*

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We can use an **ellipsis** to cut out parts of a quotation:

* *"I ran around the track incredibly fast."*
* *"I ran . . . incredibly fast."*

However, we shouldn't just cut anything out. When paraphrasing, it's important to keep the *meaning* of the quotation basically the same. 

What we *don't* want is for the paraphrased quote to be confusing or missing critical information.

The paraphrased quotation that makes sense and keeps its original meaning is

>**"Instead of letting books grow moldy . . . it is better to let them wear out by being read.”**

choices.content: "Instead of letting books grow moldy . . . it is better to let them wear out by being read.”
"Instead of letting books grow . . . far from the vulgar gaze, it is better to let them wear out . . .”
"Instead of . . . behind an iron grating . . . let them wear out by being read.”

assessment_items.question_content:
**Choose the best way to paraphrase the quotation.**

>"Practicing an art, no matter how well or badly, is a way to make your soul grow.” - *Kurt Vonnegut*

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We can use an **ellipsis** to cut out parts of a quotation:

* *"I ran around the track incredibly fast."*
* *"I ran . . . incredibly fast."*

However, we shouldn't just cut anything out. When paraphrasing, it's important to keep the *meaning* of the quotation basically the same. 

What we *don't* want is for the paraphrased quote to be confusing or missing critical information.

The paraphrased quotation that makes sense and keeps its original meaning is

>**"Practicing an art . . . is a way to make your soul grow.”**

choices.content: "Practicing . . . badly, is a way to make your soul grow.”
"Practicing an art . . . is a way to make your soul grow.”
"Practicing . . . make your soul grow.”

assessment_items.question_content:
**Choose the best way to paraphrase the quotation.**

>"Any book that helps a child to form a habit of reading, to make reading one of his deep and continuing needs, is good for him.” - *Maya Angelou*

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We can use an **ellipsis** to cut out parts of a quotation:

* *"I ran around the track incredibly fast."*
* *"I ran . . . incredibly fast."*

However, we shouldn't just cut anything out. When paraphrasing, it's important to keep the *meaning* of the quotation basically the same. 

What we *don't* want is for the paraphrased quote to be confusing or missing critical information.

The paraphrased quotation that makes sense and keeps its original meaning is

>**"Any book that helps a child to form a habit of reading . . . is good for him.”**

choices.content: "Any book that helps . . . one of his deep and continuing needs, is good for him.”
"Any book that helps a child to form . . . his deep and continuing needs, is good for him.”
"Any book that helps a child to form a habit of reading . . . is good for him.”

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which choice best uses the ellipsis to show a natural pause in speech?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
When used in dialogue, an **ellipsis** represents a pause in speech.

Pauses in speech can have many uses, but two common ones are

* to show uncertainty
 
 *"So . . . what should we do next?"*

* to show emphasis or build suspense

 *"And the winner is . . . Corey!"*

We should avoid adding pauses to dialogue that don't serve a clear purpose. And remember: our goal is to mirror the way people really talk!

"Hmmm" is used to show uncertainty, which we can emphasize by inserting a pause:

>**"Hmmm . . . do you really think so?"**

choices.content: "Hmmm . . . do you really think so?"
"Hmmm do you really think . . . so?"

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which choice best uses the ellipsis to show a natural pause in speech?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
When used in dialogue, an **ellipsis** represents a pause in speech.

Pauses in speech can have many uses, but two common ones are

* to show uncertainty
 
 *"So . . . what should we do next?"*

* to show emphasis or build suspense

 *"And the winner is . . . Corey!"*

We should avoid adding pauses to dialogue that don't serve a clear purpose. And remember: our goal is to mirror the way people really talk!

In this dialogue, Dracula is emphasizing the word "juice" for dramatic effect (he doesn't drink juice, but instead drinks *blood*). We can create this emphasis by pausing right before the word "juice":

>**“I never drink . . . juice,” said Dracula, his teeth glinting red in the candlelight.**

choices.content: “I never drink . . . juice,” said Dracula, his teeth glinting red in the candlelight.
“I never . . . drink juice,” said Dracula, his teeth glinting red in the candlelight.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which choice best uses the ellipsis to show a natural pause in speech?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
When used in dialogue, an **ellipsis** represents a pause in speech.

Pauses in speech can have many uses, but two common ones are

* to show uncertainty
 
 *"So . . . what should we do next?"*

* to show emphasis or build suspense

 *"And the winner is . . . Corey!"*

We should avoid adding pauses to dialogue that don't serve a clear purpose. And remember: our goal is to mirror the way people really talk!

Professor Turnip is expressing uncertainty about *when* she saw him, which we can emphasize by placing the pause right before "yesterday":

>**“I thought I saw him . . . yesterday, maybe?” said Professor Turnip, scratching her head.**

choices.content: “I thought I saw . . . him yesterday, maybe?” said Professor Turnip, scratching her head.
“I thought I saw him . . . yesterday, maybe?” said Professor Turnip, scratching her head.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which choice best uses the ellipsis to show a natural pause in speech?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
When used in dialogue, an **ellipsis** represents a pause in speech.

Pauses in speech can have many uses, but two common ones are

* to show uncertainty
 
 *"So . . . what should we do next?"*

* to show emphasis or build suspense

 *"And the winner is . . . Corey!"*

We should avoid adding pauses to dialogue that don't serve a clear purpose. And remember: our goal is to mirror the way people really talk!

Jamilah is expressing doubt, so we can place the pause *after* the word "sure" to show her trailing off in a disbelieving way:

>**“You saw a ghost in the refrigerator? Sure. . ." said Jamilah, rolling her eyes.**

choices.content: “You saw a ghost in the refrigerator? Sure. . ." said Jamilah, rolling her eyes.
“You saw a ghost in the . . . refrigerator? Sure," said Jamilah, rolling her eyes.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which choice best uses the ellipsis to show a natural pause in speech?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
When used in dialogue, an **ellipsis** represents a pause in speech.

Pauses in speech can have many uses, but two common ones are

* to show uncertainty
 
 *"So . . . what should we do next?"*

* to show emphasis or build suspense

 *"And the winner is . . . Corey!"*

We should avoid adding pauses to dialogue that don't serve a clear purpose. And remember: our goal is to mirror the way people really talk!

This dialogue expresses doubt and uncertainty, so we can introduce the statement by placing a pause after "well":

>**"Well . . . I'm not sure I can make it. I'm attending my cousin's wedding that day."**

choices.content: "Well, I'm not sure I can make it. I'm attending my cousin's . . . wedding that day."
"Well . . . I'm not sure I can make it. I'm attending my cousin's wedding that day."

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which choice best uses the ellipsis to show a natural pause in speech?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
When used in dialogue, an **ellipsis** represents a pause in speech.

Pauses in speech can have many uses, but two common ones are

* to show uncertainty
 
 *"So . . . what should we do next?"*

* to show emphasis or build suspense

 *"And the winner is . . . Corey!"*

We should avoid adding pauses to dialogue that don't serve a clear purpose. And remember: our goal is to mirror the way people really talk!

In this dialogue, a story trails off before a question is interjected. To show this, we can place a pause right before the question:

>**"So you were walking down the road . . . and then what happened?"**

choices.content: "So you were walking down the road . . . and then what happened?"
"So you were walking down the . . . road, and then what happened?"
